edges.description: VHL loss prevents ubiquitination of HIF-alpha subunits
Stabilized HIF-alpha accumulates and activates target genes
HIF activates VEGF transcription driving neovascularization
HIF activates genes promoting proliferation and altered metabolism
Neovascularization supports tumor growth and progression
Growth factors and metabolic changes drive tumorigenesis
VHL loss and HIF activation predispose renal tubular epithelium to clear cell RCC.
Highly vascular CNS hemangioblastomas are part of the VHL tumor spectrum.
Retinal capillary hemangioblastomas are part of the VHL vascular tumor spectrum.
VHL-associated tumor susceptibility includes adrenal and extra-adrenal chromaffin tumors.
VHL tumor susceptibility includes pancreatic endocrine/neuroendocrine tumors.
VHL-associated renal lesions include cystic kidney disease.
VHL-associated pancreatobiliary lesions include pancreatic cystic disease.
VHL tumor susceptibility can involve the endolymphatic sac of the inner ear.

nodes.description: Hemangioblastomas of the cerebellum are the most common CNS manifestation, occurring in 60-80% of VHL patients. These highly vascular tumors may cause symptoms through mass effect or associated cyst formation.

Clear cell RCC occurs in approximately 70% of VHL patients. Tumors are often bilateral and multifocal. Mean age of diagnosis is 39 years, compared to 62 years for sporadic RCC. Leading cause of mortality in VHL patients.

evidence.reference_title: Belzutifan for Renal Cell Carcinoma in von Hippel-Lindau Disease.
evidence.snippet: Patients with von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease have a high incidence of renal cell carcinoma owing to VHL gene inactivation and constitutive activation of the transcription factor hypoxia-inducible factor 2α (HIF-2α).

nodes.description: Stabilized HIF-alpha (primarily HIF-2alpha in RCC) dimerizes with HIF-1beta (ARNT) and translocates to the nucleus where it activates transcription of hypoxia-responsive genes. This occurs constitutively regardless of oxygen levels, creating a pseudo-hypoxic state.

evidence.reference_title: Up-regulation of hypoxia-inducible factors HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha under normoxic conditions in renal carcinoma cells by von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor gene loss of function.
evidence.snippet: in VHL deficient cell lines, both HIF-alpha subunits are constitutively expressed, whereas re-introduction of a functional VHL gene restores the instability of HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha proteins under normoxic conditions.

nodes.description: Endolymphatic sac tumors are inner-ear neoplasms within the VHL tumor spectrum.

HIF activates transcription of genes including PDGF, TGF-alpha, EGFR, GLUT1, and glycolytic enzymes. This promotes cell proliferation, survival, and a shift toward glycolytic metabolism (Warburg effect) that supports tumor growth.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau disease-related neoplasia with an emphasis on renal manifestations.
evidence.snippet: immunohistochemical studies for markers such as paired box 8 (PAX-8), carbonic anhydrase 9 (CA9), and glucose transporter 1 (GLUT-1) have an important role in routine clinical practice and represent cost-effective diagnostic tools.

nodes.description: VHL protein (pVHL) functions as the substrate recognition component of an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that targets hydroxylated HIF-alpha subunits for proteasomal degradation under normoxic conditions. Loss of VHL function prevents HIF-alpha degradation, allowing HIF accumulation even in the presence of oxygen.

evidence.reference_title: The VHL tumor suppressor: master regulator of HIF.
evidence.snippet: The von-Hippel Lindau tumor suppressor (pVHL) is the substrate recognition component of an E3 ubiquitin ligase and functions as a master regulator of HIF activity by targeting the hydroxylated HIF-alpha subunit for ubiquitylation and rapid proteasomal degradation under normoxic conditions.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor: not only HIF's executioner.
evidence.snippet: VHL tumor suppressor is a specific substrate-recognition component of the E3 ubiquitin complex, which regulates proteasomal degradation of the subunit of the hypoxia inducible transcription factor (HIF). Impaired VHL complex function leads to accumulation of HIF, overexpression of various HIF-induced gene products and formation of highly vascular neoplasia.

nodes.description: Pancreatic cysts (serous cystadenomas) are common in VHL patients but are almost always benign. They are distinct from pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau disease-related neoplasia with an emphasis on renal manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with VHL disease develop numerous cysts and tumors involving multiple organs including the kidneys, central nervous system, endolymphatic sac, lungs, pancreatobiliary system, adrenal glands, epididymis, and/or broad ligament.

nodes.description: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors occur in 5-17% of VHL patients. Most are nonfunctional. The main concern is metastatic potential, though most are indolent. Size-based surveillance guides management.

Pheochromocytomas occur in 10-20% of VHL patients overall but are more common in Type 2 families (up to 60%). Often bilateral and may occur at young ages. Screening by plasma or urine metanephrines is recommended.
Multiple renal cysts are common in VHL patients and may precede RCC development. Some cysts contain foci of clear cell RCC. Surveillance imaging monitors for solid enhancing components.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau disease-related neoplasia with an emphasis on renal manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with VHL disease develop numerous cysts and tumors involving multiple organs including the kidneys, central nervous system, endolymphatic sac, lungs, pancreatobiliary system, adrenal glands, epididymis, and/or broad ligament.

nodes.description: Retinal hemangioblastomas (also called retinal capillary hemangiomas) occur in 25-60% of VHL patients and may be the presenting manifestation. They can cause vision loss through exudation, hemorrhage, or retinal detachment.

The combination of increased angiogenesis, growth factor signaling, and metabolic reprogramming creates a permissive environment for tumor development. Different tissues show variable susceptibility, explaining the characteristic tumor spectrum of VHL disease including RCC, hemangioblastomas, and pheochromocytomas.

evidence.reference_title: The VHL tumor suppressor: master regulator of HIF.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in pVHL can be found in familial and sporadic hemangioblastomas, clear cell carcinomas of the kidney, pheochromocytomas and inherited forms of erythrocytosis, illustrating the importance of disrupted molecular oxygen sensing in the pathogenesis of these diseases.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor: not only HIF's executioner.
evidence.snippet: Loss of von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) protein function results in an autosomal-dominant cancer syndrome known as VHL disease, which manifests as angiomas of the retina, hemangioblastomas of the central nervous system, renal clear-cell carcinomas and pheochromocytomas.

nodes.description: VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) is a direct HIF target gene. Constitutive HIF activation leads to chronic VEGF overexpression, driving extensive tumor angiogenesis. This explains the highly vascular nature of VHL-associated tumors, particularly hemangioblastomas and clear cell RCC.

biological_processes: vascular endothelial growth factor signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: Up-regulation of hypoxia-inducible factors HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha under normoxic conditions in renal carcinoma cells by von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor gene loss of function.
evidence.snippet: In a previous study of hemangioblastomas, the most frequent manifestation of hereditary von Hippel-Lindau disease (VHL), we found elevated levels of vascular endothelial growth factor and HIF-2alpha mRNA in stromal cells of the tumors.

evidence.reference_title: Up-regulation of hypoxia-inducible factors HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha under normoxic conditions in renal carcinoma cells by von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor gene loss of function.
evidence.snippet: The data presented here provide evidence for a role of the VHL protein in regulation of angiogenesis and erythropoiesis mediated by the HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha proteins.

nodes.description: Germline heterozygous VHL mutations result in one functional allele. Somatic loss or mutation of the remaining wild-type allele (second hit) eliminates VHL function, initiating tumorigenesis. This follows Knudson's two-hit hypothesis for tumor suppressor gene inactivation.

evidence.reference_title: von Hippel-Lindau disease-related neoplasia with an emphasis on renal manifestations.
evidence.snippet: von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease is characterized by biallelic inactivation of the VHL gene leading to abnormal or absent VHL protein function, and constitutive activation of hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF) that leads to pro-tumorigenic signaling.
